titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Michael Gru register of legal cases and press clippings 1992.030
titlestmt.author: Nicholas Pavlik
titlestmt.sponsor: This collection was originally processed and described as part of the project, "Uncovering the Secrets of Brooklyn's 19th Century Past: Creation to Consolidation," funded by the Council on Library and Information Resources, with additional support from The Gladys Krieble Delmas Foundation.

profiledesc.creation: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on 2026-05-19 20:06:17 +0000.
profiledesc.langusage: Description is written in: English, Latin script.

change.item: Revised by Dee Bowers to add biographical information

did.unittitle: Michael Gru register of legal cases and press clippings
did.abstract: Two-volume register of Brooklyn lawyer Michael Gru's legal cases for the period 1875 to 1900, as well as a book of press clippings regarding cases in which Gru was involved.

bioghist.head: Biographical note
bioghist.p: Michael Gruschenko was born in approximately 1854 or 1855 to Mr. and Mrs. James Gruschenko on West 31st Street in Manhattan. The family moved to Brooklyn when Michael was 8 years old. He was one of 18 children born to the couple, including his youngest brother George and his oldest brother Joseph, as well as his sister Mrs. Hannah Robinson.

Michael and George opened a law office together in Brooklyn at 26 Court Street in 1880. Both brothers started using the shortened surname Gru around 1879. Michael had been admitted to the bar in September 1873 or 1878 after attending either Columbia Law School or New York Law School (sources conflict). He had also clerked for United States Commissioner David C. Winslow, and worked with Bernard "Barney" Hughes.

In June 1889, Michael married Nancy Kaiser. Michael was described in the Brooklyn Eagle's announcement of his marriage as a "well known and popular lawyer and politician." He was active in politics and philanthropy and was a member of the Royal Arcanum, Zeredatha Lodge of Free Masons, the Elks, B and P.O. of E. No. 22, the Manhasset Club, the Union Democratic Club of the Tenth Ward, the Hebrew Orphan Asylum of Brooklyn, the Hebrew Benevolent Association, and the Home Circle. He also served as counsel for the Hamilton Co-operative Building and Loan Association as well as other State Building and Loan Associations, several furniture dealers, one of John Y. McCane's brothers, and "heirs in large estates" (Brooklyn Citizen, 1899).

Michael Gru died in June 1900 and was survived by his wife. At that time, the couple was living at 397 Union Street in Brooklyn. His funeral was conducted by Rev. Dr. Louis Pulvermacher, who was the first president of Congregation Mount Sinai. He was buried in the family plot in Washington Cemetery.

list.head: Sources
list.item: "Tenth Ward [officers]." Brooklyn Eagle, January 30, 1877, p4. Accessed via newspapers.com May 19, 2026.
"Young Lawyers - Michael Gru." Brooklyn Daily Times, March 29, 1884, p6. Accessed via newspapers.com May 19, 2026.
"The Rev. John Jones: Lawyer Michael Gru would like to meet him." Brooklyn Daily Times, December 8, 1888, p1. Accessed via newspapers.com May 19, 2026.
"Gru-Kaiser [marriage announcement]." Brooklyn Eagle, June 19, 1889, p6. Accessed via newspapers.com May 19, 2026.
"Prominent Members of the Bar of Kings County - Michael Gru." Brooklyn Citizen, December 10, 1889, p46. Accessed via newspapers.com May 19, 2026.
[Legal notice regarding Michael Gru estate case]. Brooklyn Citizen, July 20, 1896, p8. Accessed via newspapers.com May 19, 2026.
[Michael Gru death notice]. Brooklyn Eagle, June 8, 1900, p6. Accessed via newspapers.com May 19, 2026.
[Michael Gru death notice]. Brooklyn Citizen, June 9, 1900, p2. Accessed via newspapers.com May 19, 2026.
"Funeral of Michael Gru." Brooklyn Eagle, June 11, 1900, p2. Accessed via newspapers.com May 19, 2026.
"57 Years a Practicing Lawyer is the Record of George Gru, 78." Brooklyn Times Union, June 28, 1936, p12. Accessed via newspapers.com May 19, 2026.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Identification of item, date (if known); Michael Gru register of legal cases and press clippings, 1992.030, Box and Folder number; Brooklyn Public Library, Center for Brooklyn History.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Minimally processed to the collection level.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Finding aid revised by Dee Bowers in May 2026 to incorporate biographical information from research conducted by Kevina Tidwell.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The materials in this collection are in the public domain. While many items at the Center for Brooklyn History are unrestricted, we do not own reproduction rights to all materials. Be aware of the several kinds of rights that might apply: copyright, licensing and trademarks. The researcher assumes all responsibility for copyright questions.

controlaccess.subject: Actions and defenses -- New York (State) -- Kings County
